title: Thousands of apps, a handful of models
content: Only a few organizations train frontier models, because doing so is extremely expensive. Yet there are thousands of AI products: writing tools, support bots, coding assistants, and invoice readers. Smaller teams can still ship AI products because they do not need to train the model themselves.

title: The AI API: intelligence from a socket
content: An API lets a developer's code send a prompt to a provider's model over the internet and get the response back, usually paying by token usage. It works like other infrastructure services: most companies do not build the foundation themselves, they connect to it and use it as needed.
next: The flow is simpler than you might think.

title: Developer, API, model, response
content: The chain: the developer's app sends a request to the provider's API - say OpenAI's or Anthropic's. The API runs the prompt through the model and returns the text. The app then does whatever it wants with that answer: shows it, stores it, acts on it. Every AI product you have used that is not ChatGPT or Claude itself was almost certainly built this way.
next: And what developers build with it is usually not an agent.

title: The workflow: a fixed assembly line
content: A workflow is a predetermined sequence of steps where an LLM handles one or a few of them. The order is fixed; the model does not decide what happens next. It acts more like one station in an assembly line than an autonomous worker. This is one of the most common patterns in business AI.
next: One concrete example makes it click.

title: Example: the invoice pipeline
content: An email arrives. The LLM extracts invoice details such as sender, amount, and due date from messy text. The amount is saved to the accounting database. A confirmation reply is sent, and a notification appears in Slack. This is a multi-step workflow with one AI step inside it, not an autonomous agent.
next: So when do you need an agent, and when a workflow?

title: Recipe or chef?
content: A workflow is like a recipe: fixed steps, same order each time, predictable result. An agent is more like a chef given a goal and freedom to improvise the steps. If the path is known in advance - invoices, ticket routing, or report generation - the recipe is usually better. Reserve the chef for more open-ended problems.
next: In production, the recipe usually wins - for good reasons.

title: Why workflows win at work
content: Workflows are predictable, testable, cheaper, and easier to debug. Each step can be checked separately, and failures are easier to localize. When a company says it 'uses AI', the reality is often a workflow like the invoice example. That is not a lesser form of AI; it is simply the more practical pattern in many production settings.
next: Step back, and all your building blocks form one picture.

title: The assembly kit
content: You now hold the full kit: a model accessed via an API, shaped by prompts, extended with tools, grounded by RAG, personalized by memory - arranged either as a fixed workflow or a free agent loop. Every AI product you encounter, from a support bot to a coding assistant, is some arrangement of exactly these pieces.
next: One question remains: with so many models on offer, which one goes in the kit?
